exemplar.title: To what extent is the sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County in Kenya, influenced by wind processes, soil acidity, and human and environmental conditions?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is the sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County in Kenya, influenced by wind processes, soil acidity, and human and environmental conditions?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical, rooted in coastal geomorphology theory and supports testable hypotheses
Precise location (Mambrui dunes, Kilifi County) with clear feasibility for primary data collection
Logical linkage to syllabus content and three wellâformulated hypotheses
weaknesses: Hand-drawn maps lack a scale bar and grid reference, reducing cartographic precision
Minor grammatical imprecision in the research topic statement
Introduction could integrate coastal geomorphology theory more explicitly
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (question formulation and location specificity) fully meet the topâband descriptors; only the mapping strand falls slightly short due to missing conventions. Overall performance warrants the maximum holistic mark of 3 for Criterion A.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Comprehensive, step-by-step descriptions of wind, pH and vegetation measurement procedures
Clear justification for choice of digital instruments and systematic transect sampling
Thorough pH meter procedure enhancing reproducibility
weaknesses: Limited replication and depth in data collection reduce overall reliability
Contradictory or unclear wording (e.g., âadverse weather conditions were favourableâ)
Missing calibration steps and occasional step-numbering errors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method descriptions are detailed and clearly aligned with the research question (top band), but only moderate data depth and reliability were achieved (second band), yielding a holistic score of 2 for Criterion B.

strengths: All collected variables are directly relevant and sufficient for robust hypothesis testing
Use of multiple appropriate presentation techniques (tables, line graphs, photographs, correlation table)
Conventions (figure numbers, captions, units) are largely adhered to
weaknesses: Inconsistent graph scaling and axis labeling hinder clarity
Typographical errors in captions and labels (e.g., missing 'Fig. 5', misspellings)
Omission of units in some tables
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are relevant and wellâpresented using suitable techniques, with only occasional convention errors. The information treatment meets the 5â6 band descriptors, meriting 5 marks for Criterion C.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Clear identification of trends and anomalies with explicit linking to hypotheses
Integration of one statistical test (Spearmanâs rank) alongside descriptive analysis
Good use of coastal theory to explain anomalies, enhancing interpretation
weaknesses: Statistical depth is limited to a single test without confidence intervals or error bars
Links between human-activity data and theory remain somewhat superficial
Minor gaps in completeness of evidence for some variables
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The written analysis demonstrates strong descriptive and statistical techniques with clear links to the research question and theory. Although there are minor gaps and missing uncertainty measures, the work fits the 5â6 band at the upper end, justifying 6 marks for Criterion D.

strengths: Conclusion revisits all three hypotheses with clear summarization of core findings
Arguments fully supported by the data trends identified in analysis
weaknesses: Conclusion is concise but could further contextualize findings within broader coastal theory
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear and fully supported by the analysis, meeting the topâband descriptor for Criterion E and earning 2 marks.

strengths: Relevant strengths and weaknesses of the methodology are outlined with explanation
Practical improvement suggestions are provided with brief justification of their impact
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks deeper discussion of methodological implications
Improvement suggestions could be expanded with more detailed impact analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines strengths/weaknesses and suggests relevant improvements, aligning with the band 2 descriptor for Criterion F and receiving 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two hand-drawn location maps are provided with north arrow and key, but scale bars are missing and coastal orientation is only roughly sketched.  While they do locate the study site, cartographic conventions are only partly followed, fitting the middle band description of âmap included but with excess detail or lacking conventionsâ.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focuses on dune geomorphology and explicitly references wind, soil chemistry and human influence, all of which are rooted in syllabus content for Oceans and Coastal Margins.  The student goes on to develop three testable hypotheses that flow directly from this question, demonstrating that the wording enables hypothesis formation.  Consequently, the descriptor for the top band is met.

Data are displayed through tables, line graphs, photographs and a Spearman correlation tableâappropriate choices for the variables.  However, axis titles are sometimes confusing and graph scales inconsistent, indicating use of suitable but not the âmost effectiveâ techniques; hence the middle band.

Digital instruments and quadrat sampling are appropriate and appear to have been used correctly; however, only one set of results is presented and replication is limited, so reliability is moderate rather than âhigh qualityâ.  This aligns with the second band where techniques are correct but data depth is modest.

Descriptive commentary is coupled with one statistical test (Spearmanâs rank), fulfilling the requirement for both descriptive and statistical techniques.  Minor ranking inaccuracies and absence of confidence levels prevent access to the top band, so a low score within 5â6 is appropriate.

Trends and anomalies for each variable are identified and explicitly linked to the hypotheses and relevant coastal theory, but links are sometimes superficial and some variables (human activity) are less rigorously connected.  This meets the 3â4 descriptor, awarded at the upper end.

A precise locality (Mambrui dunes, Kilifi County, Kenya) is identified, supported by verbal description of proximity to the Sabaki River and coastal processes that justify feasibility of primary data collection along a 0â400 m transect.  The level of spatial detail is sufficient for replicability and demonstrates clear feasibility for primary work, matching the highest band.

Strengths and weaknesses are outlined and explained (e.g., digital equipment accuracy, tape-measure limitations, site selection issues), showing relevance but lacking depth on methodological implications, so the descriptor for an âappropriate outlineâ (band 2) is satisfied.

All collected variables (wind, pH, vegetation cover) are directly relevant and allow partial testing of each hypothesis.  Nevertheless, the dataset is small (13 points) and human-activity evidence is mainly qualitative, so sufficiency is adequate rather than complete, placing it in the 3â4 band (awarded 3).

The conclusion revisits all three hypotheses, summarises core findings and references supporting data trends, demonstrating overall consistency with the analysis.  Although concise, it is clearly supported, warranting the top band for Criterion E.

The analysis addresses each hypothesis and discusses anomalies, yet gaps remainâchiefly limited statistical treatment for wind and vegetation and no quantitative analysis of human impact.  Therefore the work aligns with âanalysis with some gaps in evidenceâ (3â4 band, upper score).

Procedures for measuring wind speed, soil pH and vegetation cover are step-by-step, justified and clearly related to the research question.  Pilot study, transect design and systematic sampling are explained, and theoretical reasons for choosing each variable are stated, satisfying the descriptor for comprehensive explanation and clear alignment.

Most figures include figure numbers and captions, units are generally shown, and methods follow IB conventions, yet several typographical errors (e.g. mis-labelled graph titles, inconsistent decimal places) persist.  Such occasional mistakes place convention adherence in the 3â4 mark range.

Two practical improvement suggestions (measuring wheel, selecting a less disturbed site) are provided with brief justification of their likely impact on data quality, fulfilling the criteria for ârelevant improvement suggestions outlinedâ, i.e., band 2.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, explicitly references wind processes, soil acidity and human influence, and supports hypothesis formation. This strong alignment with IB Oceans and Coastal Margins theory enhances the investigationâs focus.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The research topic statement is clear but could be refined grammatically (e.g., replace âand environmental conditionsâ with âenvironmental factorsâ) to improve precision and readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The hand-drawn map locates Mambrui but lacks a scale bar. Including a scale and grid reference would strengthen spatial representation and adherence to cartographic conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The introduction definitions are accurate but generic. Integrating coastal geomorphology theory (e.g., aeolian transport mechanisms) here would better contextualize the investigation within the syllabus.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context narrative is thorough, but the paragraph could be streamlined for clarity. Consider bullet points or subheadings to separate fluvial, aeolian and human influences.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The sentence âmany types of dunes are a result of sand wave formationâ reads awkwardly. Can the student clarify whether waves or aeolian processes dominate dune genesis here?
The three hypotheses are well formulated, specific and directly testable. This clear theoretical linkage supports robust data analysis later in the report.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: In section 1.2 the word âinvestigatioâ is missing an ân.â Consistent spelling and grammar are essential for criterion C conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The study-area figure caption reads âEnd point. Area of focus Mambrut...â which is unclear. Revise to describe the transect start/end clearly and label key features.
The pilot study description is helpful; however, detail the sampling decisions (e.g., why choose those instrument models) to strengthen the methodological justification.
The phrase âadverse weather conditions were favourableâ is contradictory. Clarify whether weather aided or hindered data collection for transparency.
The systematic sampling along transect points is well described and aligns clearly with the research question, ensuring spatial coverage of dune characteristics.
In the wind-measurement procedure the instruction âRepeat steps 3 to 5â is incorrect. Ensure step numbering matches actions to avoid confusion during fieldwork.
The list of equipment for wind speed measurement is appropriate and justified, showing clear relevance to the fieldwork question.
Include a calibration step for the digital pH meter (e.g., buffer solutions) to ensure measurement accuracy across different soil samples.
Fig. 5 caption is unnumbered. Add âFig. 5â before the description to maintain consistency in figure conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The pH sampling procedure is thorough and systematic, which will yield reliable data across the transect. The detailed steps aid reproducibility.
The qualitative description of human activities is insightful but could be strengthened by assigning categories or ranking frequency to quantify their impact.
The vegetation cover formula is correctly formatted, but note explicitly that the total area is 1 mÂ² to avoid any ambiguity.
Table 1 presents wind speed data clearly and systematically along the transect, directly supporting the hypothesis and enabling trend analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Graph 1âs axis labels vary in scale increments. Use consistent interval spacing and label both axes with units to improve clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Consider adding confidence intervals or error bars when interpreting the windâdistance relationship to quantify uncertainty and strengthen statistical linking.
The term âdesstanceâ is misspelled; correct to âdistance.â Accurate spelling ensures clarity in technical interpretations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpretation of anomalies is sound, but explicitly reference coastal dynamic theory (e.g., local topography effects) to deepen the geospatial link.
The soil acidity table omits unit labels for the acidity values. Adding âpHâ to the heading will clarify data interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The anomaly explanation for pH at 200 m is well linked to organic matter processes, demonstrating strong data interpretation and linking to environmental theory.
